coredata.dc:title: Tryptophan-functionalized graphene quantum dots with enhanced curcumin loading capacity and pH-sensitive release
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Ghanbari, N.
Salehi, Z.
Khodadadi, A.A.
Shokrgozar, M.A.
Saboury, A.A.
Farzaneh, F.
coredata.dc:description: Tryptophan-conjugated graphene quantum dots (Trp-GQDs) were fabricated for the first time and loaded with curcumin (Cur) as a hydrophobic anticancer agent, to evaluate Cur loading capacity and release kinetics, as well as its cytotoxicity effect on human breast cancer cells. The GQDs were synthesized through a facile oxidation method, Trp was conjugated onto GQDs via amide bonds and Cur was loaded onto GQDs and Trp-GQDs by non-covalent interactions. TEM, FESEM, PL, UV–Vis, and FTIR analysis were used to study the nano-assemblies structural and spectral characterizations. The drug loading capacity increased by increasing the initial concentration of Cur, and by 23% after the conjugation of GQDs with Trp. A pH-sensitive release was also detected at pH 5.5 and 7.4. Moreover, the experimental release data of Cur from the two nano-assemblies at both pHs had the best compliance with the pseudo-second-order model. In-vitro cellular cytotoxicity on MCF-7 cells demonstrated the nontoxicity of the bare GQDs and Trp-GQDs nanocarriers. Considering the biocompatibility, traceability, high drug loading capacity, and pH-sensitivity of Trp-GQDs nanocarrier, it can be concluded that Cur/Trp-GQDs can be regarded as a promising candidate for drug delivery applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Tryptophan
Curcumin
Cancer
Drug loading capacity

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Release of Cur from Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs
Kinetics of Cur Release from Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the chemical functionalization of GQDs with Trp and their further loading by Cur to prepare Cur/Trp-GQDs.
(a) TEM image of GQDs, (b) FESEM image of GQDs, (c) Particle size of GQDs determined by DLS with a hydrodynamic size of 24.4 nm.

PL emission spectra of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths (as the excitation wavelength changes from 460 nm to 490 nm the emission peak correspondingly shows an obvious red-shift from 526 to 540 nm along the arrow, and the PL intensity decreases obviously). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs, Trp, Trp-GQDs, Cur, Cur/GQDs, and Cur/Trp-GQDs (b) FTIR spectra of GQDs, Trp, Trp-GQDs, Cur, Cur/GQDs, and Cur/Trp-GQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs and Cur/GQDs at the same concentration of GQDs at an excitation wavelength of 430 nm (b) FESEM image of Cur/GQDs (c) drug loading yield (%Y) of Cur on GQDs at different initial concentrations of Cur and fixed GQDs concentration of 1 mg/ml.
Cumulative release of Cur from (a) Cur/GQDs and (b) Cur/Trp-GQDs at pH values of 5.5 and 7.4.

(a) In vitro cytotoxicity of GQDs and Trp-GQDs following the MTT assay. (b) Anticancer activity of Cur/Trp-GQDs, Cur/GQDs, and free Cur following the MTT assay. MCF-7 cells were treated with different drug concentrations for 48 h.
DLS hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of GQDs and Cur/GQDs.
Drug loading capacity (%DLC), drug encapsulation efficiency (%EE) and drug loading yield (%Y) of Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs at a Cur initial concentration of 1.25 mg/ml.

Regression parameters (R2 & RMSE) obtained by fitting the data for the Cur release from Cur/Trp-GQDs nano-assembly to the first order, pseudo-second-order, and Higuchi kinetics models at pH 5.5 and pH 7.4.

head.ce:title: Tryptophan-functionalized graphene quantum dots with enhanced curcumin loading capacity and pH-sensitive release

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Cell Bank of Iran, Pasteur Institute of Iran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Cell Bank of Iran, Pasteur Institute of Iran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biochemistry and Biophysics, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biochemistry and Biophysics, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Preventative Gynecology Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Preventative Gynecology Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tryptophan-conjugated graphene quantum dots (Trp-GQDs) were fabricated for the first time and loaded with curcumin (Cur) as a hydrophobic anticancer agent, to evaluate Cur loading capacity and release kinetics, as well as its cytotoxicity effect on human breast cancer cells. The GQDs were synthesized through a facile oxidation method, Trp was conjugated onto GQDs via amide bonds and Cur was loaded onto GQDs and Trp-GQDs by non-covalent interactions. TEM, FESEM, PL, UV–Vis, and FTIR analysis were used to study the nano-assemblies structural and spectral characterizations. The drug loading capacity increased by increasing the initial concentration of Cur, and by 23% after the conjugation of GQDs with Trp. A pH-sensitive release was also detected at pH 5.5 and 7.4. Moreover, the experimental release data of Cur from the two nano-assemblies at both pHs had the best compliance with the pseudo-second-order model. In-vitro cellular cytotoxicity on MCF-7 cells demonstrated the nontoxicity of the bare GQDs and Trp-GQDs nanocarriers. Considering the biocompatibility, traceability, high drug loading capacity, and pH-sensitivity of Trp-GQDs nanocarrier, it can be concluded that Cur/Trp-GQDs can be regarded as a promising candidate for drug delivery applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent progress in nanotechnology, especially in the biomedical field, led to the design of various drug delivery systems, which have presented high potential for use in different methods of cancer diagnosis and therapies [1,2]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new generation of carbon-based nanomaterials, are among the most effective nanocarriers due to their outstanding physicochemical and biological characteristics, such as stable photoluminescence, high surface area, excellent biocompatibility, good bioavailability, and proper stability [3]. However, all these properties can be tuned or enhanced by functionalization [4–9].

GQDs have abundant surface functional groups on their surface and edges, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy, which are suitable for further functionalization through covalent conjugations, ionic interactions, and hydrogen bonds. Possessing a large number of delocalized π electrons as well as a nearly planar structure, GQDs have two prevalent requirements of establishing π-π interactions with anticancer drugs having an aromatic ring. Since drug loading capacity is a crucial factor for an efficient nanocarrier, surface modification of GQDs is beneficial for enhancing this parameter [10–12].

More recent studies report several materials that have been used for the functionalization of GQDs [13–15]. The surface functionalization of GQDs with these agents can efficiently enhance the GQDs solubility, sustainability, biocompatibility, and optical performance, such as increase in photostability and photoluminescence quantum yields [6].

Covalent functionalization of GQDs via amide bond formation with an amine-containing aromatic species can lead to increase in the total number of aromatic rings and possibly enhancement of the drug loading capacity of GQDs. To examine this assumption, tryptophan (Trp), as an essential cyclic amino acid, was selected for conjugation onto GQDs. Trp has proper solubility and biocompatibility, as well as antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties [16]. Furthermore, with the capability of absorption and emission in the UV region, Trp is the dominant source of fluorescence in proteins [17]. Considering the advantages of both GQDs and Trp, the conjugation of Trp to GQDs forms an improved nanocarrier with appropriate properties for loading and release of Curcumin (Cur) as an anticancer agent.

Cur was chosen as a model anticancer agent to be loaded onto Trp-GQDs. Its anticancer ability is highly promising in controlling various cancers [18–21]. However, the Cur clinical application is hindered due to its poor water solubility, low bioavailability and rapid metabolism [22], which can be overcome by loading it onto a nanocarrier to be used as a drug delivery system [23–25]. Up till now, there is no report using Trp as a functional agent to enhance GQDs characteristics for drug delivery applications.

In this work, Trp was covalently conjugated onto GQDs via amide bonds and used as nanocarriers for Cur as an anticancer drug against MCF-7 cells. GQDs were prepared by disintegration of graphene oxide in the presence of hydrogen peroxide and ammonia, and functionalized by Trp via amide covalent bonds using EDC/NHS activators (Fig. 1 ). Trp is a fluorescent, biocompatible, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory molecule, and its aromatic ring can enhance Cur loading by hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions. The release kinetics and its pH sensitivity were also investigated. Finally, the cytotoxicity of the nanocarriers was studied against the breast cancer cell line by MTT assay.

Graphite powder was purchased from Fluka. Potassium permanganate, sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, hydrogen peroxide, ammonium solution, Trp, Cur, and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Merck Millipore. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), fetal bovine serum (FBS), 0.25% (w/v) trypsin, and RPMI-1640 medium were purchased from Gibco BRL (Carlsbad, CA). The dialysis membrane (3.5 kDa) was obtained from Spectrum Labs Co (USA), and the Amicon Ultra centrifugal filter units (10 kDa and 3 kDa) were purchased from Merck Millipore. The human breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) was obtained from the cell bank of Pasteur Institute, Tehran, Iran. All solutions were prepared in deionized water or phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) unless it is specified.

The morphological and structural characteristics of the samples were examined with transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips, CM300, Netherland) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss, SIGMA VP-50, Germany). Dilute aqueous dispersion of GQDs were drop-casted on the carbon-coated copper grid and silicon wafer for TEM and FESEM analyses, respectively. The hydrodynamic size distribution as well as zeta-potentials were measured with a dynamic light scattering (DLS) method (ZetaSizer, Malvern Instruments, Nano ZS90, USA) using a sonicated diluted aqueous dispersion of samples. For investigating the optical properties of the samples, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis, Varian, Carry 100 Bio, Australia) absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL, Varian, Carry eclipse, Australia) emission spectra were conducted. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR, Bruker, Vector 22, Germany) spectra were obtained using a KBr pellet method. The crystalline phases of the GQDs was determined using X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (XRD, Philips PW 1730/10, Netherlands).

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized using the improved Hummers’ method [26,27]. In brief, after gradually adding a mixture of H2SO4/H3PO4 to graphite powder (1.0 g), KMnO4 (6.5 g), as an oxidizing agent, was added to the mixture placed in an ice bath for keeping the temperature below 20 °C. In the next step, the mixture was heated to 37 °C, followed by a two-day continuous stirring using a stirrer (500 rpm). When the reacted mixture cooled to the ambient temperature, hydrogen peroxide was added to it dropwise until the mixture color changed into sharp yellow, which is a sign of completing the reaction. The synthesized GO was then washed with HCl (0.1 M) to eliminate any residual ions and acids. Finally, the obtained GO was washed with DI water and ethanol several times using centrifugation (4000 rpm, 20 min).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized through a clean and fast method [28]. In a typical procedure, an aqueous dispersion of GO was sonicated for 30 min at room temperature to become homogenous. H2O2 (40 ml, 30%) was then added to GO (3 ml, 8 mg/ml) and stirred continuously at 80 °C. Afterward, ammonia (7 ml, 25–28%) was added dropwise to the mixture, which was accompanied by vigorous bubbling and heating up. After stirring for 24 h, a clear homogenous yellowish-brown mixture was finally obtained with no detectable or precipitated GO remaining. After filtering the as-prepared mixture through a 0.22 μm cellulose acetate membrane, an Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter (10 kDa) was used to remove too small particles. The remaining mixture on the filter containing GQDs was further washed with DI several times to become neutral and stored at 4 °C in the dark.

Trp-GQDs were prepared by conjugating the amino group of Trp with the carboxyl group of GQDs, using EDC/NHS according to a previously reported technique [29]. Firstly, EDC (48 mg) and NHS (16 mg), as cross-linking agents, were added simultaneously to the GQDs mixture (6 ml, 0.5 mg/ml). After stirring for 2 h, which is enough for activating the carboxyl group of GQDs, 2 mg of Trp was introduced to the above mixture at room temperature. Stirring was performed overnight for completing the reaction. Trp-GQDs was obtained after eliminating the free Trp and other impurities using an Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter (3 kDa).

Simple mixing was applied for the loading of Cur onto GQDs nanocarriers. As soon as Cur was dissolved in ethanol (1.5 ml, 1.25 mg/ml), it was added to the GQDs dispersion (1.5 ml, 1 mg/ml) with a 50:50 (v/v) mixture of ethanol and water. The mixture was then stirred in the dark at room temperature for 24 h. The final product was washed repeatedly with solvent using the Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter (3 kDa) to remove free Cur molecules. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 430 nm was used to calculate the drug loading capacity (%DLC), drug encapsulation efficiency (%EE) and drug loading yield (%Y) using indirect method [30]:
(1)
%DLC =
W total Cur
−
W free Cur
W total GQDs
× 100
(2)
%EE =
W total Cur
−
W free Cur
W total Cur
× 100
(3)
%Y =
W total Cur
−
W free Cur
W total Cur
+
W total GQDs
× 100
where Wtotal Cur and Wtotal GQDs are the initial weights of Cur and GQDs, respectively, and Wfree Cur is the weight of unloaded Cur after equilibration. In the next step, the effect of Cur initial concentration on drug loading capacity was studied by repeating the experiment at different Cur initial concentrations (0.15, 0.31, 0.62, 1.25, 2.50 mg/ml) and the fixed GQDs concentration (1 mg/ml). Free Cur concentration on the filtrate was also measured according to Cur standard calibration curve. The same procedure was done for the loading of Cur on Trp-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release of Cur from Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The behavior of Cur release from the two nano-assemblies of Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs was evaluated using the membrane dialysis method. In a typical procedure, 5 ml of each of Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs samples were placed into the dialysis tubing (3.5 kDa). After checking for sealing, the dialysis tubing was immersed into a PBS (50 ml, 150 mM, pH 7.4, and 37 °C) containing dark vessel, under a continuous moderate shaking (140 rpm). Due to the low solubility of Cur, 0.5% w/v tween-80 was added to the release media to facilitate Cur release in buffer solution [25]. For determining the Cur release profile, 2.5 ml of release media was taken out at certain time intervals to calculate its Cur amount via UV–Vis analysis using a calibration curve. It should be mentioned that the removed Cur was subsequently refilled with the fresh one to accelerate the release process in the new equilibrium condition. The following equation is consequently used to calculate the cumulative Cur release [31]:
(4)
Cumulative release
( % )
=
C t
V 0
+
V '
∑
i = 1
t − 1
C i
M total
× 100
where Mtotal is the initial amount of Cur; V0 and V' are the total volumes of the release media and the volume of the removed samples at certain time intervals, respectively; Ct is the calculated Cur concentration in the release media at time t. A similar experiment was conducted for a pH value of 5.5.

MCF-7, the human breast cancer cells, were selected for cell viability investigations. The cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10% FBS in a humidified atmosphere (5% CO2) at 37 °C.

In order to investigate the effect of various formulations of Cur (free Cur, Cur/GQDs, Cur/Trp-GQDs) as well as the GQDs and Trp-GQDs as drug-free nanocarriers on MCF-7 cell viability, the MTT colorimetric assay was performed as a common and accessible in-vitro cytotoxicity method. Firstly, 50 μl of cell suspension was cultured in a 96-well plate at a density of 4 × 103 cells per well. Then, the cells were treated with 100 μl of free Cur, Cur/GQDs, and Cur/Trp-GQDs (final Cur concentrations: 50, 25, 12.5 and 6.25 μg/ml) as well as GQDs and Trp-GQDs (50, 25, 12.5, 6.25 μg/ml), taking the untreated cells as control. After 48 h of incubation, 100 μl of the MTT stock solution was added to wells followed by 4 h incubation at 37 °C in the dark. Then, the medium was precisely removed for further addition of isopropanol to dissolve the formazan crystals made by the living cells through the reduction of MTT dye. After measuring the absorbance, the relative cell viability was determined using the following formula [32]:
(5)
Cell viability
( % )
=
OD Treated
OD Control
× 100

GQDs were synthesized through a facile method under the synergistic effect of hydrogen peroxide and ammonia. H2O2, as a strong oxidant, had the role of degrading and cutting down the GO into smaller size GQDs and NH3 was used for amine functionalization of GQDs. Briefly, the free radical of hydroxyl generated from the decomposition of H2O2 attacked the GO sheets and disintegrated them into smaller size GQDs. In contrast with conventional solvents and oxidants which are not environmentally friendly, H2O2 produces green by-products of O2 and H2O, which is highly promising, especially for biomedical applications [28,33]. TEM and FESEM images (Fig. 2 a and b) show the morphology and size of the prepared GQDs. The TEM image demonstrates that the GQDs are mostly quasi-hexagonal in shape with high transparency due to their low atomic thickness. In addition, the approximate dimensions detected from TEM are within the range of 20–30 nm, which is also consistent with the GQDs FESEM image. These observations are further supported by DLS results, showing the hydrodynamic size and size distribution of aqueous dispersions of GQDs. As indicated in Fig. 2c and Table 1 , the hydrodynamic size of GQDs is measured as 24.4 nm, which complies well with previous reports [34,35]. The synthesized GQDs have plenty of oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl and show a negative (-20.7 mV) zeta potential (Fig. S1) in their aqueous dispersion. This causes charge repulsion between GQDs nanoparticles which makes GQDs highly soluble and stable in an aqueous system. XRD was applied to investigate the crystal structure of the GQDs. As shown in Fig. S2, the XRD pattern of the GQDs exhibits a peak near 2θ value of 23.49°, which corresponds to d-spacing of 3.78 Å of (002) plane of graphite. Another peak is observed at 32.23° which is attributed to (100) plane of hexagonal carbon (JCPDS No. 41-1487) [36]. Hence, the crystal structure of the GQDs is confirmed by XRD.

To explore the optical and photo-physical properties of GQDs, UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy were applied. Fig. 3 shows that the emissions of GQDs aqueous dispersion are broad, and its location is strongly dependent on the excitation wavelength, as also reported in the literature [33]. When the excitation wavelength varies from 460 to 490 nm, the photoluminescence peak correspondingly shows an obvious red-shift from 526 to 540 nm, and the PL intensity decreases obviously [37]. The strongest emission spectrum of GQDs is at 526 nm (λem) when excited at a wavelength of 460 nm (λex). The origination of GQDs photoluminescence could be attributed to the oxygen-containing functional groups at the free zigzag edges with carbene-like triplet ground state [38]. Also, several kinds of research have revealed the effect of nitrogen groups and C–N configuration on the electronic structure of GQDs and, consequently, their photoluminescence [39]. Fig. 4 a illustrates the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs with a characteristic peak at around 232 nm, which is attributed to π-π* transition of aromatic sp
2 domains [39–42].

FTIR spectroscopy is a significant tool for determining the structural and surface functional groups of materials. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 4b) of the synthesized GQDs, two broad peaks within the range of 3000–3235 cm−1 are observed, which are assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups. The CO stretching vibration of carboxyl groups is detected at 1723 cm−1, and the CC stretching vibration is detected at 1618 cm−1 [43]. The presence of peaks at 1440 cm−1 and 1300 cm−1 are assigned to the N–H bending and O–H bending vibrations, respectively. The peak detected at 1109 cm−1 is associated with C–N stretching groups. FTIR spectra verify the existence of various oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups in GQDs, which facilitate its further functionalizations.

The yellowish-brown solution of GQDs turned into the pale yellow upon conjugating with Trp, which is evidence of the proper modification process. As both GQDs and Trp have carboxylic acid and amine groups, the conjugation of GQDs with Trp was carried out through an EDC/NHS cross-linking method in which Trp was attached to GQDs via amide bond formation between carboxylic acid and amine groups of GQDs and Trp. The carboxylic acid group was first activated with EDC to form an active ester between GQDs and Trp, which was then stabilized by NHS. To ensure binding of as high carboxylic groups of GQDs as possible, an excess amount of Trp was used [44]. Fig. 4a presents the UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs, Trp, and Trp-GQDs. The characteristic peak of Trp at 279 nm, attributed to the π-π* transitions of the indole group of Trp [45], is presented at UV–Vis spectra of Trp-GQDs with a slight shift which confirms the successful conjugation [46]. The proper conjugation of Trp to GQDs was also proved by FTIR spectroscopy. In the spectrum of Trp-GQDs (Fig. 4b), the CO bond stretching vibration of the amide group (-CO–N–H-) appears at 1643 cm−1 [47]. Also, the disappearance of the peak of Trp at 744 cm−1 attributed to the out plane N–H bending vibration is the evidence that the carboxylic groups of GQDs reacted with the NH2 groups in Trp to form an amide linkage [44]. The representative peaks of asymmetric stretching of CH3 and symmetric stretching of CH2 appear at 2963 cm−1, and 2932 cm−1 becomes more intense, which is another indication of the interaction of Trp with GQDs. The peaks of Trp presented at 1592 cm−1, 1412 cm−1, 1352 cm−1, 1231 cm−1, and 1099 cm−1, which are assigned to CO stretching, N–H bending, O–H bending, C–O stretching and C–N, respectively, shift to the position of 1558 cm−1, 1448 cm−1, 1386 cm−1, 1261 cm−1 and 1084 cm−1 in Trp-GQDs, confirming the conjugation of Trp to GQDs.

Cur was loaded onto GQDs nanocarrier via simple mixing at room temperature, which are not proper conditions for covalent conjugation [48]. Given the sp2-hybridized graphitic π conjugated structure of GQDs, they can form π–π stacking and/or hydrophobic interaction with an aromatic anticancer agent, Cur. Furthermore, amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups of GQDs can also form a strong hydrogen-bonding interaction with hydroxyl groups in Cur. Upon loading of Cur onto GQDs, the individual characteristic peak of Cur at 430 nm are detected in Cur/GQDs with a clear red-shift at 441 nm, as can be observed in Fig. 4a. This can be attributed to an increase in the chain length of the double bond conjugation and the ground-state electron donor-acceptor interaction between GQDs and Cur [47]. The UV–Vis absorption results completely confirm the above-mentioned hypothesis about the mechanism of Cur loading onto GQDs. As can be observed in Fig. 5 a, at an excitation wavelength of 430 nm, GQDs show a maximum photoluminescence emission at 540 nm. However, in Cur/GQDs at the same concentration and excitation wavelength, a remarkable quenching of GQDs photoluminescence emission was detected. This may result from the strong π–π stacking and hydrophobic interaction between GQDs and Cur molecules, which cause the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) along with the GQDs interface or a photoinduced electron-transfer process [48]. The photoluminescence quenching was also reported for other graphitic structures when interacting with aromatic or double bond conjugated materials [49,50]. The formation of Cur/GQDs was further confirmed by the FTIR spectra of Cur, GQDs, and Cur/GQDs, as presented in Fig. 4b. It is supposed that Cur is absorbed on GQDs through hydrogen bonding interaction between amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of GQDs and hydroxyl groups of Cur as well as π-π stacking between aromatic groups of GQDs and Cur. This is supported by the appearance of Cur peaks of 1625 cm−1, 1508 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 1271 cm−1 with a slight red-shift at a wavelength of 1639 cm−1, 1523 cm−1, 1388 cm−1, and 1274 cm−1, respectively. Increase in the peak intensity of the common groups such as CH3 and CH2 at 2928 cm−1 and 2851 cm−1 is another demonstration of the loading of Cur onto GQDs. The DLS and zeta potential of Cur/GQDs also vary remarkably compared to GQDs. As presented in Table 1, the size of the Cur/GQDs increases to 77.5 nm, which shows the absorption of Cur molecules onto the surface of GQDs. The zeta potential of Cur/GQDs also becomes more negative (−32 mV), which is expected considering the oxygen-containing groups of Cur. According to the FESEM image in Fig. 5b, the size of GQDs increases dramatically after the absorption of Cur molecules, which additionally confirms the Cur loading onto GQDs. The Cur loading yield (%Y) was also measured by UV–Vis at 430 nm at various initial concentrations of Cur (0.15, 0.31, 1.25, 2.50 mg/ml) and the constant concentration of GQDs (1 mg/ml). According to Fig. 5c, as the initial concentration of Cur increases, the encapsulation efficiency decreases while the drug loading yield increases; this is in agreement with the similar studies [51]. The changes in Cur encapsulation efficiency and Cur loading yield become slow with increase in the initial concentration of Cur from 1.25 mg/ml to 2.5 mg/ml. Moreover, the prepared Cur/GQDs at the Cur initial concentration of 2.5 mg/ml was highly turbid and unstable, unlike those prepared at lower Cur initial concentrations. Considering all these points together, the Cur initial concentration of 1.25 mg/ml was selected to be used for the rest of the experiments. Cur loading capacity (%DLC), Cur encapsulation efficiency (%EE), and Cur loading yield (%Y) of Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs at Cur initial concentration of 1.25 mg/ml are presented in Table 2 .

Fig. 4a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cur/Trp-GQDs compared to Cur and Trp-GQDs. The gradual red-shifting of the absorption peak of Cur at 430 nm towards 441 nm in Cur/Trp-GQDs can be attributed to the strong π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interaction between Trp-GQDs and Cur. The FTIR spectroscopy was used to confirm the preparation of Cur/Trp-GQDs (Fig. 4b). Appearance of three peaks at 799 cm−1, 1264 cm−1 and 1558 cm−1 in spectrum of Cur/Trp-GQDs, corresponding to the out plane N–H bending, C–O stretching, and CO stretching of Trp-GQDs, respectively, as well as absorption peaks at 663 cm−1 and 877 cm−1 from the C–H stretching vibrations of Cur, confirm the loading of Cur onto Trp-GQDs. The intensified peaks of Cur/Trp-GQDs at 1384 cm−1, 2927 cm−1 and 2864 cm−1 belonging to the common groups of Cur and Trp-GQDs which are attributed to the O–H bending, asymmetric stretching of CH3, and symmetric stretching of CH2, respectively, are another evidences of proper loading of Cur onto Trp-GQDs through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interaction.

Considering the acidic condition of tumor microenvironment compared to normal tissues, which is neutral, applying a pH-sensitive drug delivery system can be beneficial for the controlled release of drugs. To study the pH-sensitivity and sustained drug release, the Cur release profiles from GQDs and Trp-GQDs nanocarriers were determined at 37 °C at two pH values of 7.4 and 5.5 as a representative of physiological pH of whole-body fluid and intracellular lysosomes and endosomes, respectively [52,53]. As indicated in Fig. 6 , no distinct burst release is observed for the two nano-assemblies during the first 7 h, considering the cumulative Cur release amounts below 17% for Cur/GQDs and below 8% for Cur/Trp-GQDs at both pH values. It was also observed that pH variation does not significantly affect the Cur release during the first hours. This might be explained by the fact that at the very beginning of the experiment, those Cur molecules start to release from the system, which are located on the exterior layers of the nano-assemblies with no specific interactions. For Cur/GQDs (Fig. 6a) after 150 h, up to 47% of Cur was released at pH 5.5, which is approximately two times the amount released at pH 7.4 at the same period of time. However, the Cur release from the Cur/Trp-GQDs (Fig. 6b) nano-assembly demonstrated a slower release rate in comparison to that from Cur/GQDs, indicating that Trp could cap the GQDs to slow down Cur release. For Cur/Trp-GQDs, the amounts of Cur release within 150 h are 34% and 19% at pH 5.5 and 7.4, respectively. Thus, for both nano-assemblies, the amount of Cur release is higher in acidic media compared to neutral media, similar to previous reports [54,55]. It is supposed that Cur was loaded onto Trp-GQDs through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions, both of which are affected by pH variations [32,37]. The π-π stacking is between aromatic groups of GQDs and Cur. In contrast, hydrogen bonding is probable between any hydrogen-containing group and oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups of Cur and Trp-GQDs. As reported earlier, under acidic conditions, the hydrophobic drugs become protonated, leading to weakening of the π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions between drug molecules and the graphene surface. This results in more release at acidic condition [52]. On the contrary, these interactions have excellent stability under physiological conditions, causing both nano-assemblies of Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs to be stable within the whole body's blood circulation with neutral pH. Once their cellular uptake occurs, the acidic condition of the endosomes and lysosomes facilitates the Cur rate of release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of Cur Release from Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Kinetics of Cur Release from the two nano-assemblies at the two pH values of 5.5 and 7.4 were determined by regression analysis of experimental data using first order (Eq. (6)) [56], pseudo-second-order (Eq. (7)) [57], and Higuchi (Eq. (8)) [56] models:
(6)
Log Q t
=
Log Q 0
−
k 1
t
2.303
(7)
t
Q t
=
1
k 2
Q e 2
+
t
Q e
(8)
Q t
=
k H
t 0.5
where Q0, Qe, and Qt are the initial, equilibrium, and instantaneous amount of Cur released, respectively, and k is the release rate constant. The model fitting results are reported in Table 3 , which clearly proves the compliance of the experimental data with the pseudo-second-order model for both nano-assemblies at the two pH values. The plot of linear relationship for each of the models is shown in Fig. S3.

One of the crucial prerequisites of applying nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems in biomedical fields is the biosafety and non-toxicity of the nanocarrier in addition to efficient cytotoxicity of the drug-loaded nanocarrier. To achieve this purpose, MTT assay was used as a potent and common in-vitro cytotoxicity test. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, GQDs and Trp-GQDs nanocarriers show almost no cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells after 48 h incubation with cell viability of above 94% for concentrations as high as 50 μg/ml. These findings are in a good agreement with previously reported results in literature [58–61]. The MTT assay results of treating MCF-7 cells for 48 h with Cur, Cur/GQDs, and Cur/Trp-GQDs with an equal Cur content also revealed a Cur dosage-dependent behavior, as indicated in Fig. 7b. Furthermore, higher cytotoxicity is observed for free Cur rather than the other two nano-assemblies of Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs. This indicates that Cur should be released from Cur/GQDs and Cur/Trp-GQDs after their cellular uptake, which is undoubtedly more complicated than free diffusion of Cur molecules to MCF-7 cancerous cells. It can also be observed that Cur/Trp-GQDs is more cytotoxic against MCF-7 cells at all concentrations compared to Cur/GQDs. There is a hypothesis that tumor cells increase their tryptophan consumption to evade the immune system [62]. Considering this assumption, Cur/Trp-GQDs can be internalized better by MCF-7 cancerous cells, due to the existence of tryptophan in its structure, leading to its higher cytotoxicity.

In the present study, GQDs were synthesized, conjugated with Trp, and loaded with Cur as an anticancer agent, to mainly examine the effect of Trp on the drug loading capacity of GQDs as well as its impact on drug release kinetics and cytotoxicity effect on human breast cancer cells. Trp was anchored to GQDs via amide covalent bonds followed by a non-covalent loading of Cur to prepare the Cur/Trp-GQDs nano-assembly. A 23% higher drug loading capacity was observed for Trp-GQDs compared to bare GQDs. This was due to the fact that the conjugation of Trp, as an aromatic agent, to GQDs led to an increase in the total number of aromatic structures of GQDs. This resulted in increased π-π stacking interactions with Cur as an aromatic drug and ultimately enhanced Trp-GQDs drug loading capacity. The nano-assembly revealed a pH-sensitive and sustained drug release with a 100% higher release rate in acidic media compared to neutral condition. This can be attributed to the weakening of the H-bonding interaction between Cur and the nanocarrier as a result of protonation of the OH group of Cur under the pH value of 5.5. The experimental release data also fitted very well to the pseudo-second-order model. The results of MTT cytotoxicity assay confirmed the nontoxicity of GQDs and Trp-GQDs nanocarriers against MCF-7 cells with the cell viability of above 92% at a concentration as high as 50 μg/ml. Nevertheless, considerable cytotoxicity was detected for the two Cur-loaded nanocarriers, especially for Cur/Trp-GQDs. In conclusion, the synthesized Trp-GQDs nanocarrier show enhanced drug loading capacity, traceability, high biocompatibility, and pH-sensitivity. In addition, when loaded by Cur, it shows a desirable antitumor activity. Therefore, the developed Cur/Trp-GQDs nano-assembly offers the superior potential for cancer therapy.
This study was financially supported by grant No. 961202 of the Biotechnology Development Council of the Islamic Republic of Iran.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no competing interests associated with this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Preventative Gynecology Research Center of Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences for their financial supports.
